title: Clyde Fernald (1907-1992): A Life Rooted in Troy, Maine
narrative: Clyde Fernald was born on 5 September 1907 in Troy, a small farming town tucked into the rolling country at the heart of Waldo County, Maine. He was the son of Medwin Joseph Fernald and Eldora "Dora" Meservie, and he belonged to the Fernald family that had long made its home in Troy. The town into which he was born was an old one by Maine standards: first settled around 1801 as Bridge's Plantation, it was incorporated in 1812 as Kingville, briefly renamed Joy in 1815 and Montgomery in 1826, and finally took the name Troy later that same year. By the time Clyde was a boy, Troy had already passed its population peak and was a quiet, close-knit rural community of fewer than eight hundred people.

The Maine in which Clyde grew up was a landscape of small, diversified family farms. Waldo County households of the early twentieth century typically raised a little of everything to get by, mixing hay, potatoes, apples, sweet corn, and poultry with milk, eggs, and butter from a few dairy cows. Much of what these farms produced fed the family first, with the surplus sold to nearby stores and local markets. It was a hardworking, largely self-sufficient way of life, shaped by long winters, short growing seasons, and the steady rhythm of planting and harvest. Census records show Troy's population slipping from 768 in 1910 to 638 by 1920 as young people drifted toward cities and better-paying work, a slow rural decline that formed the backdrop of Clyde's youth.

Clyde married Marion F. Bragg, who came from another established local family; her parents were Oscar and Katie Bragg. Together Clyde and Marion raised their children in Troy. Family records and a surviving obituary for their daughter Kay Frances Fernald Tozier (born in Troy in 1932) confirm that Clyde and Marion were her parents and that the children's paternal grandparents were Medwin and Dora Fernald. Kay's account names at least three children of Clyde and Marion who reached adulthood: Kay herself and sons James Fernald and Stephen Fernald, both of whom remained tied to the Troy area. The Fernald and Bragg names recur throughout the records of the town across these generations.

The documentary trail for an ordinary rural Mainer of Clyde's era is, by its nature, thin. We can be confident of the framework of his life, his birth in Troy, his parentage, his marriage to Marion Bragg, and the children they raised, but the day-to-day texture of his working years is not preserved in the free public record. Given the agricultural character of Troy and the family's long presence there, it is reasonable to picture him in the local mold of a farming and laboring household, though the specific trades he followed are not documented in the sources available here. Where the record is silent, this account stays silent rather than inventing detail.

Clyde lived nearly his whole life within a few miles of where he was born. He died on 14 December 1992 in Albion, in neighboring Kennebec County, just across the line from his native Troy, at the age of 85. His was a life spent almost entirely in the central Maine countryside, spanning the better part of the twentieth century, from the horse-and-wagon farming world of 1907 through the Great Depression, two world wars, and into the modern era. He was remembered through his descendants, who carried the Fernald name forward in the same Waldo County towns where he had begun.

Note on sources: the core facts of Clyde's life, his dates, his parents, his wife, and his children, are corroborated by family records and by the published obituary of his daughter Kay Fernald Tozier, which independently names Clyde and Marion Bragg Fernald as her parents and Medwin and Dora Fernald as her grandparents. The surrounding picture of Troy and of early-twentieth-century Waldo County farming is drawn from the town's published history and from Maine agricultural history. No portrait of Clyde could be located in free, public-domain sources, so none is shown.

sources.title: Kay Fernald Tozier obituary (names parents Clyde and Marion Bragg Fernald, grandparents Medwin and Dora Fernald), Bangor Daily News
Troy, Maine - Wikipedia (incorporation history, geography, census population 1900-1940)
Troy, Waldo County, Maine Genealogy - FamilySearch Wiki
Troy, Waldo County - Maine Genealogy (town history and records)
Farming in Maine - Maine Memory Network / Maine History Online (early-20th-century agriculture)
James Joseph Fernald obituary (brother of Kay; confirms Fernald family of Troy), PenBay Pilot

images.caption: Context, not a portrait of Clyde: children gathering potatoes on a Maine farm, October 1940. Photograph by Jack Delano for the U.S. Farm Security Administration (Library of Congress); public domain. Shown to illustrate the kind of small-farm rural life common in central Maine during Clyde Fernald's working years.
images.credit: Jack Delano, U.S. Farm Security Administration / Library of Congress (public domain)
